name: lambda
context: Create, deploy, and invoke AWS Lambda serverless functions
info: AWS Lambda runs code without provisioning or managing servers. You upload a function handler, configure triggers (API Gateway, S3 events, SQS, schedules), and Lambda executes your code in response to events. Lambda scales automatically from zero to thousands of concurrent executions. Supported runtimes include Node.js, Python, Java, Go, .NET, and custom runtimes via containers. The AWS SDK v3 `@aws-sdk/client-lambda` package provides Invoke, CreateFunction, UpdateFunctionCode, and other commands. Lambda integrates with [[aws/s3]] for event-driven file processing, [[aws/iam]] for execution roles, and [[aws/cloudformation]] for deployment.
body:
# AWS Lambda

Lambda is AWS's serverless compute service. Write a function handler, upload your code, and Lambda runs it in response to events — no servers to manage. You pay only for the compute time consumed, billed in 1ms increments.

## Create and Deploy

```javascript
import { LambdaClient, CreateFunctionCommand } from "@aws-sdk/client-lambda";
import { readFileSync } from "fs";

const lambda = new LambdaClient({ region: process.env.AWS_REGION });

await lambda.send(new CreateFunctionCommand({
  FunctionName: "my-function",
  Runtime: "nodejs20.x",
  Role: "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/lambda-execution-role",
  Handler: "index.handler",
  Code: { ZipFile: readFileSync("./function.zip") },
  Timeout: 30,
  MemorySize: 256,
}));
```

The execution role must have the permissions your function needs. Create it via [[aws/iam#roles]] with a trust policy allowing `lambda.amazonaws.com`.

## Invoke

```javascript
import { InvokeCommand } from "@aws-sdk/client-lambda";

const response = await lambda.send(new InvokeCommand({
  FunctionName: "my-function",
  Payload: JSON.stringify({ key: "value" }),
  InvocationType: "RequestResponse", // synchronous
}));

const result = JSON.parse(Buffer.from(response.Payload).toString());
console.log(result); // function return value
```

Invocation types:
- **RequestResponse** — synchronous, waits for the function to complete
- **Event** — asynchronous, returns immediately, function runs in background
- **DryRun** — validates the request without executing

## Layers

Layers let you package shared dependencies separately from function code:

- Common libraries (e.g., AWS SDK extensions, utility packages)
- Shared configuration files
- Custom runtimes

A function can use up to 5 layers. Total unzipped size (function + layers) must be under 250 MB.

## Event Sources

Lambda can be triggered by many AWS services:

| Source | Use Case |
|--------|----------|
| API Gateway | HTTP endpoints |
| S3 | File upload/delete events (see [[aws/s3]]) |
| SQS | Message queue processing |
| DynamoDB Streams | Database change events |
| EventBridge | Scheduled or custom events |
| CloudWatch Logs | Log processing |

Configure event source mappings via [[aws/cloudformation]] or the Lambda console.

## Cold Starts

When Lambda creates a new execution environment, there is a one-time initialization delay (cold start):

- **Node.js** — typically 100-500ms cold start
- **Python** — typically 100-500ms cold start
- **Java** — typically 1-5s cold start (JVM startup)
- **Provisioned Concurrency** — pre-warms environments to eliminate cold starts for latency-sensitive workloads

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` | IAM access key for the Lambda client |
| `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | IAM secret key for the Lambda client |
| `AWS_REGION` | Target region for Lambda deployment |

sections.title: Create and deploy
sections.body: Use `CreateFunctionCommand` to create a new function. Provide runtime (nodejs20.x, python3.12, etc.), handler entry point, IAM execution role (see [[aws/iam#roles]]), and zipped code. Use `UpdateFunctionCodeCommand` for subsequent deployments. For infrastructure-as-code, define functions in [[aws/cloudformation]] templates.

sections.title: Invoke functions
sections.body: Use `InvokeCommand` with `FunctionName` and `Payload`. Set `InvocationType` to `RequestResponse` for synchronous calls or `Event` for async fire-and-forget. Parse the response Payload from a Uint8Array. Use `LogType: 'Tail'` to include execution logs in the response.

sections.title: Layers and dependencies
sections.body: Package shared code and dependencies as Lambda layers. Layers are versioned and can be shared across functions and accounts. Use layers for common libraries, custom runtimes, or configuration. A function can reference up to 5 layers. Total unzipped deployment size (code + layers) must stay under 250 MB.

sections.title: Event sources and triggers
sections.body: Lambda integrates with API Gateway (HTTP), [[aws/s3]] (object events), SQS (messages), DynamoDB Streams (data changes), EventBridge (schedules and custom events), and CloudWatch Logs. Configure event source mappings to connect triggers to functions. Use [[aws/cloudformation]] to define triggers declaratively.

sections.title: Cold starts and performance
sections.body: Cold starts occur when Lambda initializes a new execution environment. Node.js and Python have 100-500ms cold starts. Java can take 1-5 seconds. Minimize cold starts by keeping function packages small, using provisioned concurrency for critical paths, and choosing lightweight runtimes. Warm functions reuse existing environments.

links.context: Execution roles define what the Lambda function can access
S3 events can trigger Lambda for file processing

Process uploaded files via S3-to-Lambda events

related.note: Lambda functions require an IAM execution role
S3 is a common event source for Lambda file processing pipelines
Use CloudFormation to deploy Lambda functions as infrastructure

scripts.name: Invoke Lambda
scripts.description: Create, deploy, and invoke an AWS Lambda serverless function

AWS_REGION.description: AWS region for the Lambda client

functionName.description: Name or ARN of the Lambda function

payload.description: JSON payload to pass to the function

statusCode.description: HTTP status code of the invocation

logResult.description: Base64-encoded execution logs (when LogType is Tail)

depends.reason: An IAM execution role is required for Lambda to access other AWS services
